sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Chest infection is a broad term covering lower respiratory tract infection, including acute bronchitis and pneumonia. In the prehospital setting, the key tasks are recognising respiratory compromise, sepsis, or alternate serious diagnoses, then providing supportive care and appropriate escalation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Cough, which may be dry or productive
- Fever, rigors, malaise, or pleuritic chest discomfort
- Dyspnoea, tachypnoea, or increased work of breathing
- Wheeze or crackles on auscultation
- Confusion, hypoxia, or hypotension in more severe infection

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider pneumonia, sepsis, asthma or COPD exacerbation, pulmonary embolus, heart failure, or acute coronary syndrome where clinically relevant
- Abnormal vital signs, focal chest findings, hypoxia, altered mental state, chest pain, or hypotension increase concern for significant illness
- Cough suppressants and routine antibiotics are not standard prehospital treatment for uncomplicated viral illness
- Escalate early if there are features of sepsis, respiratory failure, dehydration, or inability to maintain oral intake

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey and full vital sign survey
- Auscultate the chest where practicable and assess work of breathing, hydration, and conscious state
- Administer oxygen only if clinically indicated
- Encourage oral fluids only if safe, tolerated, and there is no respiratory compromise
- Consider simple antipyretic or analgesic treatment for discomfort or fever
- Escalate or arrange transport according to respiratory distress, sepsis risk, comorbidity, and local disposition pathways

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ECG if chest pain, arrhythmia, significant tachycardia, or alternate cardiopulmonary pathology is suspected
- Establish IV access only when clinically indicated and give fluids cautiously with reassessment for fluid overload
- If severe infection, sepsis, or prolonged transport is likely, discuss destination planning and any doctor-directed antimicrobial strategy according to local protocol
- Pre-notify receiving services for significant respiratory compromise, suspected sepsis, or frail high-risk patients with deterioration

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Routine antibiotics are not indicated for uncomplicated acute bronchitis
- Persistent haemoptysis, severe pleuritic pain, focal neurology, or significant deterioration should prompt urgent reassessment
